question: Is Novo Nordisk's revenue decline (-7.6% YoY) temporary or structural?

findings.claim: Semaglutide patents expired in India (March 20, 2026), with generics launched at 70-80% discounts by 5+ manufacturers
findings.data: India patent expired March 20, 2026. At least 5 domestic drugmakers launched generics undercutting Novo's price by up to 80%. 50+ generic brands expected in coming months. Indian market represents only a small fraction of Novo's revenue, but the precedent matters. Manufacturers: Zydus, Lupin, Dr. Reddy's among them.

findings.claim: Semaglutide patents also expiring in 2026 in Canada, Brazil, China, and Turkey — collectively 40% of world population
findings.data: Patent expiry timeline: India (March 2026), Brazil (March 2026), Canada (2026-2027, after Novo failed to pay maintenance fees), China (2026-2028, with 15+ Chinese companies developing generics, 11 in final-stage trials), Turkey (2027). These markets collectively represent 40% of global population and ~33% of adults with obesity. However, they are lower-revenue markets compared to US/EU.

findings.claim: US semaglutide compound patent protected until December 2031, with method-of-use patents to 2033 — no US generics before 2032
findings.data: Main US compound patent expires December 2031 (extended from March 2026 via PTA and PTE of 5+ years). Method-of-use patents extend to 2033. No generic semaglutide expected in US until 2032-2033 at earliest. EU/Japan protection also extends to 2031-2032. Novo projected to earn ~$166B from Ozempic/Wegovy during this extended protection window.

findings.claim: Lilly's tirzepatide patent protection extends into 'the back half of the 2030s' — giving Lilly a longer competitive moat
findings.data: Tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) patents extend into the late 2030s in major markets — approximately 5-7 years longer than semaglutide. This gives Lilly a structural advantage: by the time semaglutide faces US generics (2032+), tirzepatide will still be patent-protected.
